column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=CUCUMBER SOOP.
#Text=Take a houghil of beef, break it small and put it into a stew-pan, with\r
#Text=part of a neck of mutton, a little whole pepper, an onion, and a little\r
#Text=salt; cover it with water, and let it stand in the oven all night, then\r
#Text=strain it and take off the fat; pare si\r
#Text= or eight middle-siz'd\r
#Text=cucumbers, and slice them not very thin, stew them in a little butter\r
#Text=and a little whole pepper; take them out of the butter and put 'em in\r
#Text=the gravy.
#Text=Garnish your dish with raspings of bread, and serve it up\r
#Text=with toasts of bread or French roll.
#Text=To make ONION SOOP.
#Text=Take four or five large onions, pill and boil them in milk and water\r
#Text=whilst tender, (shifting them two or three times in the boiling) beat\r
#Text='em in a marble mortar to a pulp, and rub them thro' a hair-sieve, and\r
#Text=put them into a little sweet gravy; then fry a few slices of veal, and\r
#Text=two or three slices of lean bacon; beat them in a marble mortar as\r
#Text=small as forc'd-meat; put it into your stew-pan with the gravy and\r
#Text=onions, and boil them; mi\r
#Text= a spoonful of wheat-flour with a little\r
#Text=water, and put it into the soop to keep it from running; strain all\r
#Text=through a cullender, season it to your taste; then put into the dish a\r
#Text=little spinage stew'd in butter, and a little crisp bread; so serve it\r
#Text=up.
#Text=CRAW-FISH SOOP.
#Text=Take a knuckle of veal, and part of a neck of mutton to make white\r
#Text=gravy, putting in an onion, a little whole pepper and salt to your\r
#Text=taste; then take twenty crawfish, boil and beat them in a marble\r
#Text=mortar, adding thereto alittlee of the gravy; strain them and put them\r
#Text=into the gravy; also two or three pieces of white bread to thicken the\r
#Text=soop; boil twelve or fourteen of the smallest craw-fish, and put them\r
#Text=whole into the dish, with a few toasts, or French roll, which you\r
#Text=please; so serve it up.
#Text=You may make lobster soop the same way, only add into the soop the\r
#Text=seeds of the lobster.
#Text=To stew a BRISKET of BEEF.
#Text=Take the thin part of a brisket of beef, score the skin at the top;\r
#Text=cross and take off the under skin, then take out the bones, season it\r
#Text=highly with mace, a little salt, and a little whole pepper, rub it on\r
#Text=both sides, let it lay all night, make broth of the bones, skim the fat\r
#Text=clean off, put in as much water as will cover it well, let it stew over\r
#Text=a slow fire four or five hours, with a bunch of sweet herbs and an\r
#Text=onion cut in quarters; turn the beef over every hour, and when you find\r
#Text=it tender take it out of the broth and drain it very well, having made\r
#Text=a little good strong gravy.
#Text=A ragoo with sweet-breads cut into pieces, pullets tenderly boil'd and\r
#Text=cut in long pieces; take truffles and morels, if you have any\r
#Text=mushrooms, with a little claret, and throw in your beef, let it stew a\r
#Text=quarter of an hour in the ragoo, turning it over sometimes, then take\r
#Text=out your beef, and thicken your ragoo with a lump of butter and a\r
#Text=little flour.
#Text=Garnish your dish with horse-radish and pickles, lay the\r
#Text=ragoo round your beef, and a little upon the top; so serve it up.\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=12.
#Text=To stew a RUMP of BEEF.
#Text=Take a fat rump of young beef and cut off the fag end, lard the low\r
#Text=part with fat bacon, and stuff the other part with shred parsley; put\r
#Text=it into your pan with two or three quarts of water, a quart of Claret,\r
#Text=two or three anchovies, an onion, two or three blades of mace, a little\r
#Text=whole pepper, and a bunch of sweet herbs; stew it over a slow fire five\r
#Text=or si\r
#Text= hours, turning it several times in the stewing, and keep it\r
#Text=close cover'd; when your beef is enough take from it the gravy, thicken\r
#Text=part of it with a lump of butter and flour, and put it upon the dish\r
#Text=with the beef.
#Text=Garnish the dish with horse-radish and red-beet root.
#Text=There must be no salt upon the beef, only salt the gravy to your taste.
#Text=You may stew part of a brisket, or an o\r
#Text= cheek the same way.
#Text=To fry BEEF-STEAKS.
#Text=Take your beef steaks and beat them with the back of a knife, fry them\r
#Text=in butter over a quick fire, that they may be brown before they be too\r
#Text=much done; when they are enough put them into an earthen pot whilst you\r
#Text=have fry'd them all; pour out the fat, and put them into your pan with\r
#Text=a little gravy, an onion shred very small, a spoonful of catchup and a\r
#Text=little salt; thicken it with a little butter and flour, the thickness\r
#Text=of cream.
#Text=Garnish your dish with pickles.
#Text=Beef-steaks are proper for a side-dish.
#Text=How to boil a TURKEY.
#Text=When your turkey is dress'd and drawn, truss her, cut off her feet,\r
#Text=take down the breast-bone with a knife, and sew up the skin again;\r
#Text=stuff the breast with a white stuffing.
#Text=How to make the Stuffing.
#Text=Take the sweet-bread of veal, boil it,\r
#Text=shred it fine, with a little beef-suet, a handful of bread-crumbs, a\r
#Text=little lemon-peel, part of the liver, a spoonful or two of cream, with\r
#Text=nutmeg, pepper, salt, and two eggs, mi\r
#Text= all together, and stuff your\r
#Text=turkey with part of the stuffing, (the rest you may either boil or fry\r
#Text=to lay round it) dridge it with a little flour, tie it up in a cloth,\r
#Text=and boil it with milk and water: If it be a young turkey an hour will\r
#Text=boil it.
#Text=How to make Sauce for the Turkey.
#Text=Take a little small white gravy, a\r
#Text=pint of oysters, two or three spoonfuls of cream, a little juice of\r
#Text=lemon, and salt to your taste, thicken it up with flour and butter,\r
#Text=then pour it over your turkey, and serve it up; lay round your turkey\r
#Text=fry'd oysters, and the forc'd-meat.
#Text=Garnish your dish with oysters,\r
#Text=mushrooms, and slices of lemon.
#Text=To salt a Leg of Mutton to eat like Ham.
#Text=Take a leg of mutton, an ounce of saltpetre, two ounces of bay-salt,\r
#Text=rub it in very well, take a quarter of a pound of coarse sugar, mi\r
#Text= it\r
#Text=with two or three handfuls of common salt, then take and salt it very\r
#Text=well, and let it lie a week, so hang it up, and keep it for use, after\r
#Text=it is dry use it, the sooner the better; it won't keep so long as ham.
#Text=How to salt HAM or TONGUES.
#Text=Take a middling ham, two ounces of saltpetre, a quarter of a pound of\r
#Text=bay-salt, beat them together, and rub them on your ham very well,\r
#Text=before you salt it on the inside, set your salt before the fire to\r
#Text=warm; to every ham take half a pound of coarse sugar, mi\r
#Text= to it a\r
#Text=little of the salt, and rub it in very well, let it lie for a week or\r
#Text=ten days, then salt it again very well, and let it lie another week or\r
#Text=ten days, then hang it to dry, not very near the fire, nor over much in\r
#Text=the air.
#Text=Take your tongues and clean them, and cut off the root, then take two\r
#Text=ounces of saltpetre, a quarter of a pound of bay-salt well beaten,\r
#Text=three or four tongues, according as they are in bigness, lay them on a\r
#Text=thing by themselves, for if you lay them under your bacon it flats your\r
#Text=tongues, and spoils them; salt them very well, and let them lie as long\r
#Text=as the hams with the skin side downwards: You may do a rump of beef the\r
#Text=same way, only leave out the sugar.
#Text=To preserve Orange Chips to put in glasses.
#Text=Take a seville orange with a clear skin, pare it very thin from the\r
#Text=white, then take a pair of scissars and clip it very thin, and boil it\r
#Text=in water, shifting it two or three times in the boiling to take out the\r
#Text=bitter; then take half a pound of double refined sugar, boil it and\r
#Text=skim it, then put in your orange, so let it boil over a slow fire\r
#Text=whilst your syrrup be thick, and your orange look clear, then put it\r
#Text=into glasses, and cover it with papers dipt in brandy; if you have a\r
#Text=quantity of peel you must have the larger quantity of sugar.
#Text=To preserve ORANGES or LEMONS.
#Text=Take seville oranges, the largest and roughest you can get, clear of\r
#Text=spots, chip them very fine, and put them into water for two days,\r
#Text=shifting them twice or three times a day, then boil them whilst they\r
#Text=are soft: take and cut them into quarters, and take out all the pippens\r
#Text=with a penknife, so weigh them, and to every pound of orange, take a\r
#Text=pound and half of loaf sugar; put your sugar into a pan, and to every\r
#Text=pound of sugar a pint of water, set it over the fire to melt, and when\r
#Text=it boils skim it very well, then put in your oranges; if you would have\r
#Text=any of them whole, make a little hole at the top, and take out the meat\r
#Text=with a tea spoon, set your oranges over a slow fire to boil, and keep\r
#Text=them skimming all the while; keep your oranges as much as you can with\r
#Text=the skin downwards; you may cover them with a delf-plate, to bear them\r
#Text=down in the boiling; let them boil for three quarters of an hour, then\r
#Text=put them into a pot or bason, and let them stand two days covered, then\r
#Text=boil them again whilst they look clear, and the syrrup be thick, so put\r
#Text=them into a pot, and lie close over them a paper dip'd in brandy, and\r
#Text=tie a double paper at the top, set them in a cool place, and keep them\r
#Text=for use.
#Text=If you would have your oranges that are whole to look pale and\r
#Text=clear, to put in glasses, you must make a syrrup of pippen jelly; then\r
#Text=take ten or a dozen pippens, as they are of bigness, pare and slice\r
#Text=them, and boil them in as much water as will cover them till they be\r
#Text=thoroughly tender, so strain your water from the pippens through a hair\r
#Text=sieve, then strain it through a flannel bag; and to every pint of jelly\r
#Text=take a pound of double refined sugar, set it over a fire to boil, and\r
#Text=skim it, let it boil whilst it be thick, then put it into a pot and\r
#Text=cover it, but they will keep best if they be put every one in different\r
#Text=pots.
#Text=To preserve APRICOCKS.
#Text=Take apricocks before they be full ripe, stone and pare 'em; then weigh\r
#Text='em, and to every pound of apricocks take a pound of double refined\r
#Text=sugar, beat it very small, lie one part of your sugar under the\r
#Text=apricocks, and the other part at the top, let them stand all night, the\r
#Text=ne\r
#Text=t day put them in a stew-pan or brass pan; don't do over many at\r
#Text=once in your pan, for fear of breaking, let them boil over a slow fire,\r
#Text=skim them very well, and turn them two or three times in the boiling;\r
#Text=you must but about half do 'em at the first, and let them stand whilst\r
#Text=they be cool, then let them boil whilst your apricocks look clear, and\r
#Text=the syrrup thick, put them into your pots or glasses, when they are\r
#Text=cold cover them with a paper dipt in brandy, then tie another paper\r
#Text=close over your pot to keep out the air.
#Text=To preserve GOOSEBERRIES.
#Text=To a pound of ston'd gooseberries put a pound and a quarter of fine\r
#Text=sugar, wet the sugar with the gooseberry jelly; take a quart of\r
#Text=gooseberries, and two or three spoonfuls of water, boil them very\r
#Text=quick, let your sugar be melted, and then put in your gooseberries;\r
#Text=boil them till clear, which will be very quickly.
#Text=To preserve RED GOOSEBERRIES.
#Text=Take a pound of si\r
#Text=penny sugar, and a little juice of currans, put to\r
#Text=it a pound and a half of Gooseberries, and let them boil quick a\r
#Text=quarter of an hour; but if they be for jam they must boil better than\r
#Text=half an hour.
#Text=They are very proper for tarts, or to eat as sweet-meats.
#Text=To preserve BARBERRIES for TARTS.
#Text=Take barberries when full ripe, strip them, take their weight in sugar,\r
#Text=and as much water as will wet your sugar, give it a boil and skim it;\r
#Text=then put in your berries, let them boil whilst they look clear and your\r
#Text=syrrup thick, so put them into a pot, and when they are cold cover them\r
#Text=up with a paper dip'd in brandy.
#Text=To preserve DAMSINS.
#Text=Take damsins before they are full ripe, and pick them, take their\r
#Text=weight in sugar, and as much water as will wet your sugar, give it a\r
#Text=boil and skim it, then put in your damsins, let them have one scald,\r
#Text=and set them by whilst cold, then scald them again, and continue\r
#Text=scalding them twice a day whilst your syrrup looks thick, and the\r
#Text=damsins clear; you must never let them boil; do 'em in a brass pan, and\r
#Text=do not take them out in the doing; when they are enough put them into a\r
#Text=pot, and cover them up with a paper dip'd in brandy.
#Text=To dry PEARS.
#Text=Take half a peck of good baking pears, (or as many as you please) pare\r
#Text=and put them in a pot, and to a peck of pears put in two pounds of\r
#Text=sugar; you must put in no water but lie the parings on the top of your\r
#Text=pears, tie them up close, and set them in a brown bread oven; when they\r
#Text=are baked lay them in a dripping pan, and flat them a little in your\r
#Text=pan; set them in a slow oven, and turn them every day whilst they be\r
#Text=through y dry; so keep them for use.
#Text=You may dry pippens the same way, only as your turn them grate over\r
#Text=them a little sugar.
#Text=To preserve CURRANS in bunches.
#Text=Boil your sugar to the fourth degree of boiling, tie your currans up in\r
#Text=bunches, then place them in order in the sugar, and give them several\r
#Text=covered boilings, skim them quick, and let them not have above two or\r
#Text=three seethings, then skim them again, and set them into the stove in\r
#Text=the preserving pan, the ne\r
#Text=t day drain them, and dress them in bunches,\r
#Text=strow them with sugar, and dry them in a stove or in the sun.
#Text=To dry APRICOCKS.
#Text=To a pound of apricocks put three quarter of a pound of sugar, pare and\r
#Text=stone them, to a layer of fruit lie a layer of sugar, let them stand\r
#Text=till the ne\r
#Text=t day, then boil them again till they be clear, when cold\r
#Text=take them out of the syrrup, and lay them upon glasses or china, and\r
#Text=sift them over with double refined sugar, so set them on a stove to\r
#Text=dry, ne\r
#Text=t day if they be dry enough turn them and sift the other side\r
#Text=with sugar; let the stones be broke and the kernels blanch'd, and give\r
#Text=them a boil in the syrrup, then put them into the apricocks; you must\r
#Text=not do too many at a time, for fear of breaking them in the syrrup; do\r
#Text=a great many, and the more you do in it, the better they will taste.
#Text=To preserve ORANGES Whole.
#Text=Take what quantity of oranges you have a mind to preserve, chip off the\r
#Text=rind, the thiner and better, put them into water twenty-four hours, in\r
#Text=that time shift them in the water (to take off the bitter) three times;\r
#Text=you must shift them with boiling water, cold water makes them hard; put\r
#Text=double the weight of sugar for oranges, dissolve your sugar in water,\r
#Text=skim it, and clarify it with the white of an egg; before you put in\r
#Text=your oranges, boil them in syrrup three or four times, three or four\r
#Text=days betwi\r
#Text=t each time; you must take out the inmeat of the oranges\r
#Text=very clean, for fear of mudding the syrup.
#Text=To pickle SHRIMPS.
#Text=Take the largest shrimps you can get, pick them out of the shells, boil\r
#Text=them in a jill of water, or as much water as will cover them according\r
#Text=as you have a quantity of shrimps, strain them thro' a hair-sieve, then\r
#Text=put to the liquor a little spice, mace, cloves, whole pepper, white\r
#Text=wine, white wine vinegar, and a little salt to your taste; boil them\r
#Text=very well together, when it is cold put in your shrimps, they are fit\r
#Text=for use.
#Text=To pickle MUSCLES.
#Text=Wash your muscles, put them into a pan as you do your cockles, pick\r
#Text=them out of the shells, and wash them in the liquor; be sure you take\r
#Text=off the beards, so boil them in the liquor with spices, as you do your\r
#Text=cockles, only put to them a little more vinegar than you do to cockles.
#Text=To pickle WALNUTS green.
#Text=Gather walnuts when they are as you can run a pin through them, pare\r
#Text=them and put them in water, and let them lie four or five days,\r
#Text=stirring it twice a day to take out the bitter, then put them in strong\r
#Text=salt and water, let them lie a week or ten days, stirring it once or\r
#Text=twice a day, then put them in fresh salt and water, and hang them over\r
#Text=a fire, put to them a little allum, and cover them up close with vine\r
#Text=leaves, let them hang over a slow fire whilst they be green, but be\r
#Text=sure don't let them boil, when they are green pat them into a sieve to\r
#Text=drain the water from them.
#Text=To pickle large CUCUMBERS.
#Text=Take cucumbers and put them in a strong salt and water, let them lie\r
#Text=whilst they be throughly yellow, then scald them in the same salt and\r
#Text=water they lie in, set them on the fire, and scald them once a day\r
#Text=whilst they are green; take the best alegar you can get, put to it a\r
#Text=little Jamaica pepper and black pepper, some horse-radish in slices, a\r
#Text=few bay leaves, and a little dill and salt, so scald your cucumbers\r
#Text=twice or thrice in this pickle; then put them up for use.
#Text=To pickle ONIONS.
#Text=Take the smallest onions you can get, peel and put them into a large\r
#Text=quantity of fair water, let them lie two days and shift them twice a\r
#Text=day; then drain them from the water, take a little distill'd vinegar,\r
#Text=put to 'em two or three blades of mace, and a little white pepper and\r
#Text=salt, boil it, and pour it upon your onions, let them stand three days,\r
#Text=so put them into little glasses, and tie a bladder over them; they are\r
#Text=very good done with alegar; for common use, only put in Jamaica pepper\r
#Text=instead of mace.
#Text=To pickle ELDER BUDS.
#Text=Take elder buds when they are the bigness of small walnuts, lie them in\r
#Text=a strong salt and water for ten days, and then scald them in fresh salt\r
#Text=and water, put in a lump of allum, let them stand in the corner end\r
#Text=close cover'd up, and scalded once a day whilst green.
#Text=You may do radish cods or brown buds the same way.
#Text=To pickle MUSHROOMS.
#Text=Take mushrooms when fresh gather'd, sort the large ones from the\r
#Text=buttons, cut off the stalks, wash them in water with a flannel, have a\r
#Text=pan of water ready on the fire to boil 'em in, for the less they lie in\r
#Text=the water the better; let them have two or three boils over the fire,\r
#Text=then put them into a sieve, and when you have drained the water from\r
#Text=them put them into a pot, throw over them a handful of salt, stop them\r
#Text=up close with a cloth, and let them stand two or three hours on the hot\r
#Text=hearth or range end, giving your pot a shake now and then; then drain\r
#Text=the pickle from them, and lie them in a cloth for an hour or two, so\r
#Text=put into them as much distill'd vinegar as will cover them, let them\r
#Text=lie a week or ten days, then take them out, and put them in dry\r
#Text=bottles; put to them a little white pepper, salt and ginger sliced,\r
#Text=fill them up with distill'd vinegar, put over 'em a little sweet oil,\r
#Text=and cork them up close; if your vinegar be good they will keep two or\r
#Text=three years; I know it by e\r
#Text=perience.
#Text=You must be sure not to fill your bottles above three parts full, if\r
#Text=you do they will not keep.
#Text=To pickle MUSHROOMS another Way.
#Text=Take mushrooms and wash them with a flannel, throw them into water as\r
#Text=you wash them, only pick the small from the large, put them into a pot,\r
#Text=throw over them a little salt, stop up your pot close with a cloth,\r
#Text=boil them in a pot of water as you do currans when you make a jelly,\r
#Text=give them a shake now and then; you may guess when they are enough by\r
#Text=the quantity of liquor that comes from them; when you think they are\r
#Text=enough strain from them the liquor, put in a little white wine vinegar,\r
#Text=and boil it in a little mace, white pepper, Jamaica pepper, and slic'd\r
#Text=ginger; then it is cold put it to the mushrooms, bottle 'em and keep\r
#Text='em for use.
#Text=They will keep this way very well, and have more of the taste of\r
#Text=mushrooms, but they will not be altogether so white.
#Text=To pickle GARKINS.
#Text=Take garkins of the first growth, pick 'em clean, put 'em in a strong\r
#Text=salt and water, let 'em lie a week or ten days whilst they be throughly\r
#Text=yellow, then scald them in the same salt and water they lie in, scald\r
#Text=them once a day, and let them lie whilst they are green, the set them\r
#Text=in the corner end close cover'd.
#Text=To make PICKLE for your Cucumbers.
#Text=Take a little alegar, (the quantity must be equal to the quantity of\r
#Text=your cucumbers, and so must your seasoning) a little pepper, a little\r
#Text=Jamaica and long pepper, two or three shalots, a little horse-radish\r
#Text=scraped or sliced, and little salt and a bit of allum, boil them\r
#Text=altogether, and scald your cucumbers two or three times with your\r
#Text=pickle, so tie them up for use.
#Text=To pickle COLLIFLOWER white.
#Text=Take the whitest colliflower you can get, break it in pieces the\r
#Text=bigness of a mushroom; take as much distill'd vinegar as will cover it,\r
#Text=and put to it a little white pepper, two or three blades of mace, and a\r
#Text=little salt, then boil it and pour it on your colliflowers three times,\r
#Text=let it be cold, then put it into your glasses or pots, and wet a\r
#Text=bladder to tie over it to keep out the air.
#Text=To pickle Red Cabbage.
#Text=Take a red cabbage, chuse it a purple red, for the light red never\r
#Text=proves a good colour; so take your cabbage and shred it in very thin\r
#Text=slices, season it with pepper and salt very well, let it lie all night\r
#Text=upon a broad tin, or a dripping-pan; take a little alegar, put to it a\r
#Text=little Jamaica pepper, and two or three rases of ginger, boil them\r
#Text=together, and when it is cold pour it upon your cabbage, and in two or\r
#Text=three days time it will be fit for use.
#Text=You may throw a little colliflower among it, and it will turn red.
#Text=To pickle Colliflower another Way.
#Text=Take the colliflower and break it in pieces the bigness of a mushroom,\r
#Text=but leave on a short stalk with the head; take some white wine vinegar,\r
#Text=into a quart of vinegar, put si\r
#Text=-pennyworth of cochineal beat well,\r
#Text=also a little Jamaica and whole pepper, and a little salt, boil them in\r
#Text=vinegar, pour it over the colliflower hot, and let it stand two or\r
#Text=three days close covered up; you may scald it once in three days whilst\r
#Text=it be red, when it is red take it out of pickle, and wash the cochineal\r
#Text=off in the pickle, so strain it through a hair sieve, and let it stand\r
#Text=a little to settle, then put it to your colliflower again, and tie it\r
#Text=up for use; the longer it lies in the pickle the redder it will be.
#Text=To pickle BARBERRIES.
#Text=Take barberries when full ripe, put them into a pot, boil a strong salt\r
#Text=and water, then pour it on them boiling hot.
#Text=To dry CHERRIES another Way.
#Text=Take cherries when full ripe, stone them, and break 'em as little as\r
#Text=you can in the stoning; to si\r
#Text= pounds of cherries take three pounds of\r
#Text=loaf sugar, beat it, lie one part of your sugar under your cherries,\r
#Text=and the other at the top, let them stand all night, then put them into\r
#Text=your pan, and boil them pretty quick whilst your cherries change and\r
#Text=look clear, then let them stand in the syrrup all night, pour the\r
#Text=syrrup from them, and put them into a pretty large sieve, and set them\r
#Text=either in the sun or before the fire; let them stand to dry a little,\r
#Text=then lay them on white papers one by one, let them stand in the sun\r
#Text=whilst they be thoroughly dry, in the drying turn them over, then put\r
#Text=them into a little bo\r
#Text=; betwi\r
#Text=t every layer of cherries lie a paper,\r
#Text=and so do till all are in, then lie a paper at the top, and keep them\r
#Text=for use.
#Text=You must not boil them over long in the syrrup, for if it be over thick\r
#Text=it will keep them from drying; you may boil two or three pounds more\r
#Text=cherries in the syrrup after.
#Text=To preserve GRAPES another Way.
#Text=Take ripe grapes and stone them; to every pound of grapes take a pound\r
#Text=of double-refined sugar; let them stand till the sugar is dissolved;\r
#Text=boil them pretty quick till clear; then strain out the grapes, and add\r
#Text=half a pound of pippen jelly, and half a pound more sugar; boil and\r
#Text=skim it till a jelly; put in the grapes to heat; afterwards strain them\r
#Text=out, and give the jelly a boil; put it to the grapes and stir it till\r
#Text=near cold; then glass it.
